name: Sarah Napoli
title: Disability & Philanthropy Forum, Advisory Committee Reelabilities International
shortBio: Disability educator and trainer, activist and cultivator of inclusive culture
fullBio: Sarah Napoli is the learning services director at the Disability & Philanthropy Forum. From 2019-2023, she acted as the lead disability inclusion project officer within the people and culture Diversity, Inclusion, and Equity team at Open Society foundations. She has over 25 years of experience teaching and conducting training on social justice and advocacy. She holds two MA degrees in social justice in intercultural relations and applied human rights. She identifies as a proud disabled person and enjoys chatting about Geek culture and her former life as a hip hop researcher and dancer. She has conducted workshops and training all over the USA and around the globe.
altText: smiling white woman in black blazer and dress

name: Paul Behrhorst
title: Founder - ConsultAbility / Associate Director of Accessibility - PAC NYC
shortBio: Paul Behrhorst is a New York based theatre accessibility professional and co-founder of ConsultAbility, focused on making live performance more inclusive and accessible.
fullBio: Paul Behrhorst is the Associate Director of Accessibility at the Perelman Performing Arts Center (PAC NYC) and co-founder of ConsultAbility, a nonprofit focused on advancing accessibility in the arts. With over 20 years in theatre and nearly two decades specializing in accessibility, he works with artists and organizations to embed inclusive practices across productions, spaces, and audience experience. His work spans access planning, training, and consultation with institutions nationwide.

name: R. Luke DuBois
title: Faculty, NYU Ability Project
shortBio: Composer, artist, designer, and engineer working on equity and access to creative technologies at NYU.
fullBio: R. Luke DuBois creates music, art, software, and circuits, not necessarily in that order. His artwork explores the temporal, verbal, and visual structures of cultural and personal ephemera, using data-driven techniques to investigate time, memory, identity, and the meaning of portraiture in the United States in the 21st Century. His research focuses on systems for technology equity, ranging from open source software for speech to telepresent communication systems for motion capture to citizen science for noise pollution to design for disability. DuBois teaches at the NYU Tandon School of Engineering, and is a founding co-director of the NYU Ability Project.
altText: Smiling man in a black t-shirt, seated at a café.

name: Lisa B. Lewis
title: Founder/Executive Director of Omnium Circus
shortBio: Lisa is a passionate advocate and instigator of access, representation and belonging in all aspects of the arts.
fullBio: Lisa is a graduate of Ringling Bros. and Barnum & Bailey Clown College, has a BA from Brandeis University, MA in Clown/Circus History from NYU, co-founder of the Super Scientific Circus, and spent many years working with the Big Apple Circus in multiple capacities. Lisa’s passion for sharing the magic of the circus inspired her to reimagine the art form as Omnium: A Bold New Circus. Sharing in the joy and excitement of the performing arts is an experience that should be available to everyone, regardless of background, race, gender or ability and Circus Omnium makes that happen.
altText: woman with big smile and multicolored sequined vest

name: Lakshmee Lachhman-Persad
title: Accessible Travel NYC - Founder
shortBio: Founder of Accessible Travel NYC and accessibility strategist advancing disability inclusion across tourism and cultural institutions.
fullBio: Lakshmee Lachhman-Persad is an accessibility strategist, educator, and founder of Accessible Travel NYC, working at the intersection of disability, culture, and business strategy. With over 25 years across travel, tourism, and cultural sectors, she partners with organizations to embed accessibility across communications, staff development, and audience engagement—positioning it as a driver of growth and experience. A Webby Award winner, her work with institutions such as Lincoln Center, SUMMIT One Vanderbilt, and NYC Tourism advances systems change and expands access across the sector.
altText: Smiling woman of color wearing a black blazer.

name: Beth Prevor
title: Executive Director, Hands On.
shortBio: Beth is Co-Founder and Executive Director of Hands On Sign Interpreted Performances with 40+ years in cultural access.
fullBio: Beth is co-founder and Executive Director of Hands On Sign Interpreted Performances. For 40+ years, Hands On has been providing access for the Deaf community through sign language interpreted theater at some of NYC’s most prestigious theaters, On and Off Broadway. She works with arts organizations to ensure inclusion by offering community engagement and audience development strategies. She leads workshops on accessibility for organizations around the country including at the Kennedy Center’s LEAD Conference, Americans for the Arts, and Theater Communications Group. She is the 2015 recipient of the John F. Kennedy Center’s Excellence in Accessibility Leadership Lifetime Achievement award.
altText: Smiling mature white woman with short grey hair.

name: DJ McDonald
title: DJ McDonald
shortBio: DJ McDonald dreams big and works cooperatively to make the artistic experiences he envisions come to life.
fullBio: DJ McDonald has been immersed in artistic production and management involving dance, theater, film/video, music and visual arts, since he took off his jayvee hockey skates in college. As a choreographer, director and performer, he has worked with notable and peer choreographers, directors, companies, and projects as well as directing his own eponymous professional dance and theater enterprise for 15 years. He continues a 20-year relationship with the NYC Dance Parade. He dreams big and tries to find ways of working cooperatively to make things that he would like to see take place.
altText: Smiling person in light blue blazer

name: Miranda Hoffner
title: Cultural Accessibility Leader
fullBio: Miranda Hoffner has focused her career on cultural accessibility, leading accessibility at Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts and, formerly, at the Intrepid Museum. She is actively involved in the accessibility field, as member of New York City's Advisory Counsel for the Center for Workplace Accessibility and Inclusion, Queens Theatre for All Advisory Committee and Brooklyn Botanic Garden's Best Practices Working Group, and former chair of the Museum, Arts and Culture Access Consortium. Miranda's work has been recognized with the 2024 Heart of a Champion award from HeartShare Human Services and the 2020 Community CO/LABorator award from CO/LAB Theater Group.

name: Sofiya Cheyenne
title: Artist / Teaching Artist / Creative Access Consultant
fullBio: Sofiya Cheyenne is a multi-disciplinary artivist, teaching artist and creative access consultant. She believes deeply in amplifying the voices and stories of disabled peoples through art. From the education field to the entertainment industry, Sofiya merges her advocacy, artistry and facilitation skills together to challenge societal norms, combat ableism and break barriers. She is the Executive Producer of the feature documentary entitled “The Tallest Dwarf” and has produced, written and directed films including "You're Up”, “Inspiring Women” and “How We Look” all of which shine light on the little person's experience. You can learn more about Sofiya at www.sofiyacheyenne.com
altText: Sofiya smiles to the camera in a red shirt in front of grey background
